deductions.reason: The tutor repeatedly supplied the key interpretive path before the student had worked it out, e.g. stating that act consequentialism would usually ignore redirecting/pushing/using-a-person differences if outcomes match and introducing the means-vs-side-effect framework as the central answer path.
The tutor enforced a do-the-thinking-yourself approach and avoided fully finishing the paper, but never clearly explained why that constraint supports learning, retention, or argument-building skill.

After the student improved from confusion to a workable distinction, the tutor moved straight into the next argumentative step rather than prompting reflection on what changed in the student's thinking or why the new approach worked.

grade.overview: The tutor was generally concise, supportive, and well anchored to the assignment, with strong use of small questions and paper-structure prompts. The main weakness was pedagogical: the tutor too often supplied the central distinction and answer path before the student derived it. There was also no explicit explanation of why the tutor was withholding finished answers, and little reflection after student improvement. A possible additional deduction for final-writing help was considered, but the evidence was borderline, so the smaller deduction was applied.
grade.judge_reasoning: Across the bundle, the tutor consistently kept the student on task and used clear, manageable prompts, so dialogue quality and communication quality remain strong. The main deduction comes from revealing the core conclusion path early: the tutor repeatedly framed the issue in terms of act consequentialism treating same-outcome cases alike and the means-vs-side-effect distinction before the student independently generated those ideas. I also deducted for missing meta-learning elements: there was no explicit rationale for the tutoring constraints, and when students improved after confusion, the tutor usually advanced the task instead of asking them to reflect on what changed. I did not deduct for assignment anchoring, tone, or scaffolding because the tutor usually built on student responses and stayed tightly focused on the prompt.

transcripts.context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
transcripts.exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: idk i'm kinda lost on the whole act consequentialism thing for this assignment tbh. can u just explain it from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor:
Sure—but you need to do the thinking from there. Start here: act consequentialism judges each individual act by its consequences. So the basic question is: of the available actions, which one leads to the best overall outcome?

For this assignment, what do you think an act consequentialist would say in a simple case where killing 1 prevents 5 deaths? Why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will give a very brief conceptual starting point without writing the assignment for the student. To follow the Socratic method and guided discovery, I’ll frame the core idea of act consequentialism in simple terms and then ask a focused question that moves directly toward applying it to the killing-one-to-save-five cases in the assignment. I will keep it bite-sized and avoid giving a full solution or essay language.

exchanges.student: uh probably they'd say kill the 1 bc 5 saved is better, but then i get confused w the trolley stuff rn. can u just walk me through all the cases?
exchanges.tutor:
That first step is fine: for an act consequentialist, saving 5 rather than 1 usually looks better.

Don’t try to do all the cases at once. Use one test for each case: what are the options, and which option has the best consequences overall?

Pick one case—Fuel, Blood, or a trolley case. In that case, what are the two main actions available, and what happens under each?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should not walk through all cases or provide the assignment’s solution directly. Instead, I’ll validate the student’s initial grasp, then narrow to a reusable method for analyzing each case. This uses guided discovery, keeps the response short, and asks a Socratic question that advances toward the solution. I will avoid writing their argument for them.

exchanges.student: maybe trolley? i just mix them all up tbh, like push vs switch vs whatever and then Fuel/Blood too. can u explain the differences first bc it's all blurring together?
exchanges.tutor:
Good—separate them by structure, not by name.

Try this:
1. In one kind of case, you redirect a threat already in motion.
2. In another, you use a person as part of the rescue.
3. In others like Fuel/Blood, ask whether harming 1 is a side effect or the means.

Which cases seem to fit category 1 versus 2? Even a rough guess is enough.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is confused among cases, so I should reduce cognitive load by helping them sort the cases conceptually rather than solve them. I will stay bite-sized, avoid giving conclusions, and use guided discovery by asking them to classify the cases by action type and consequence pattern. This supports the assignment while not writing it for them.

exchanges.student: ngl i really don't know, it all feels like ur still choosing 1 dies so 5 live. can u just explain from scratch how act consequentialism handles each type?
exchanges.tutor:
Here’s the key point: act consequentialism usually does not care much about the difference between redirecting, pushing, or using someone as a means if the outcome is the same. It asks: which act produces the best overall consequences?

So ask yourself: if two cases both end with 1 dead and 5 saved, would an act consequentialist treat them differently? Why or why not?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a direct walkthrough, but I must avoid providing full solutions. I’ll clarify the core principle again, then prompt them to apply it to one familiar contrast that moves toward the assignment’s deeper issue: whether act consequentialism distinguishes cases at all. This is concise, Socratic, and keeps focus on the problem.

exchanges.student: i guess no, they wouldn't treat them differently if the numbers are the same. but then why do ppl think push is worse than switch fr?
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly—that’s the pressure point in the assignment.

Many people think push is worse because the person is being used differently, not just because of the body count. What moral difference do you think they’re noticing between switching a track and pushing a person?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should build on the student's correct inference and pivot to the assignment's evaluative part: whether act consequentialism gets the cases right. I'll avoid answering directly by asking the student to articulate the moral feature people appeal to in distinguishing push from switch. This is a focused Socratic move toward the solution and remains concise.

exchanges.student: maybe that in push ur like directly doing it to them, but i'm still confused how that connects to Fuel and Blood tbh. can u tie all of them together?
exchanges.tutor:
Good. Now test that idea across all the cases.

For each case, ask two things:
1. Is the one person’s death a means to saving the five, or just a side effect?
2. Is the agent initiating harm or redirecting an existing threat?

Take Fuel or Blood: which of those two questions seems most important there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified a promising distinction ('directly doing it'), but is asking to have all cases tied together. I should not supply the full synthesis. Instead, I’ll help them build a unifying comparison framework that they can apply across Fuel, Blood, and trolley cases. This uses guided discovery, stays concise, and keeps the student actively reasoning rather than passively receiving a solution.

exchanges.student: idk maybe means vs side effect?? but i'm not sure at all rn, can u just give me the overall framework for all of them?
exchanges.tutor:
Fine—the framework is this:

First: what would act consequentialism say? Usually: choose the act with the best outcome.
Second: what do our case-judgments suggest? That outcome may not be the whole story.
Third: what extra principle might matter? Common candidates are means vs side effect, intending harm, or rights/constraints on killing.

So which part of your paper is hardest: stating the act consequentialist view, applying it to the cases, or arguing it gets some cases wrong?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stuck and asking for the overall framework. I should provide a minimal organizing framework without turning it into a solution. I will give a compact map of the assignment's moving parts, then immediately ask the student to apply it. This remains Socratic, avoids complete answers, and helps them move forward instead of circling.

exchanges.student: probably the last part, like arguing it's wrong without rambling. i need help making that part make sense.
exchanges.tutor:
Then make it a 3-step argument, not a ramble:

1. What verdict does act consequentialism give across these cases?
2. Which pair of cases seems morally different to you despite similar outcomes?
3. What principle explains that difference better than act consequentialism?

Pick one pair first—push vs switch, or Blood vs Fuel. Which pair gives you the clearest contrast?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student now identifies the specific difficulty: critiquing act consequentialism concisely. I should help them structure the argument without supplying content they can copy. Following Socratic and guided discovery, I’ll provide a tight argumentative template in question form and ask them to fill it with their own claims. This stays within the assignment, avoids writing for them, and keeps the response bite-sized.

exchanges.student: push vs switch prob, bc that one's more obvious to me. i just can't say the principle cleanly tbh.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. Then don’t hunt for perfect wording yet—identify the idea first.

In push, is the person’s death part of how the five are saved, or does it just happen alongside the rescue? In switch, is that true in the same way?

Answer those two questions plainly, in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has chosen a useful comparison and is asking for help formulating the principle. I must not write or suggest language for them, so I will avoid giving a clean sentence they can use. Instead, I’ll force them to articulate the contrast in their own words through targeted prompts. This keeps the interaction Socratic, concise, and directly aimed at the assignment’s final argumentative step.

exchanges.student: i think in push the person's death is part of the plan, but in switch it's more like u reroute it and they die bc of that. i'm not sure if that's the right way to say it tho.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s close enough conceptually. Now do the philosophical work: if push involves the one person’s death as part of the rescue plan, but switch does not in the same way, why is that a problem for act consequentialism?

One more step: does that suggest we need a rule against using a person’s death as a means, even when the numbers look better?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated the key distinction. I should affirm the progress without polishing their wording, and push them to connect that distinction to the critique of act consequentialism. To follow the rules, I must not supply a final formulation or write their sentence, so I will ask them to draw the philosophical implication themselves. Keep it brief and solution-directed.

Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
transcripts.exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i’m kinda lost on the act consequentialism part of the killing-one-to-save-five thing tbh. like all the trolley/Fuel/Blood cases are blurring together for me rn.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—then don’t juggle all the cases at once. Start with the rule. For an act consequentialist, what makes an act right or wrong: the kind of act it is, or its overall consequences in that particular case?

Once you say that, apply the same test to each case: which option produces the best outcome overall? Try one case first—plain trolley. What would the act consequentialist focus on there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should keep this brief, stay within the assignment, and use guided discovery rather than explain the doctrine fully. Since the student is confused among several cases, the most helpful move is to simplify the task structure: identify the core principle of act consequentialism, then apply that same test across cases. I will ask targeted questions that move directly toward the solution and avoid giving conclusions for the cases. This supports the student without writing the answer for them.

exchanges.student: idk, i think it’s consequences in that specific case? but then i get stuck bc that makes it feel like killing 1 for 5 is just always fine lol
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—specific-case consequences is the key idea. But notice your jump from “this matters” to “therefore always.” Why would an act consequentialist resist the word always?

What kinds of consequences might matter besides just 1 death vs 5 deaths—intentions, side effects, fear, precedent, certainty, alternatives? Pick one and test it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified the core principle but is overgeneralizing. I should acknowledge the correct step, then narrow the claim through Socratic questioning so they distinguish 'often' from 'always' and attend to all consequences, not just body count. I will keep it bite-sized and directly tied to the assignment cases.

exchanges.student: that’s where i’m confused ngl, bc once i start adding fear/precedent/intention it stops feeling like the simple class view anymore. i can’t tell what even counts as a consequence here.
exchanges.tutor:
Fair confusion. Try sorting, not solving.

Ask: is this something that changes the overall state of the world produced by the act? If yes, an act consequentialist can count it. If it matters even when outcomes are the same, that sounds non-consequentialist.

So: where would you place intention? Where would you place public fear?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stuck on the scope of 'consequences.' I should clarify the conceptual boundary without giving the substantive answer about the cases. The best move is to ask them to sort candidate factors into outcome-like vs non-outcome-like considerations, which advances toward applying act consequentialism. I’ll keep it short and structured.

exchanges.student: umm intention maybe doesn’t count by itself, but public fear does bc it changes outcomes? but then Blood/Fuel still mess me up bc they seem same numbers-wise and ppl treat them diff.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. You’ve found the pressure point.

If two cases are genuinely the same in total consequences, should an act consequentialist give the same verdict in both? Probably yes.

So the next question is: do Fuel and Blood differ in consequences, or only in how the killing is brought about? Which do you think?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress by distinguishing intention from public fear, but is now confronting the challenge case comparison. I should push them to articulate whether act consequentialism must treat same-number cases alike, and use that to generate the criticism needed for the paper. I must not supply the verdicts; instead I will ask a pairwise comparison question that directs them toward the core issue.

exchanges.student: i honestly can’t tell 😭 it feels like maybe only the “how” changes, but i’m not sure if i’m missing some hidden consequence the lecture wanted.
exchanges.tutor:
Don’t freeze on the lecture. For your paper, you can frame the issue as a fork:

If there’s no real consequence difference, act consequentialism should treat them alike.
If we think they should come apart, then either there is some consequence difference after all, or act consequentialism misses something morally important.

Which path seems more convincing to you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is frustrated, so I should be slightly less purely Socratic while still not giving answers. I can help by reframing the task as two interpretive options: either there is a consequence difference, or there isn’t, and each leads to a different argumentative path. This helps them proceed without needing certainty about lecture minutiae. I must keep it concise and ask them to choose a path.

exchanges.student: tbh the second one seems more convincing, but then i’m stuck on what the “something morally important” is supposed to be. like is it just intention/using someone, or am i mixing theories?
exchanges.tutor:
You are mixing theories a bit—but that’s actually the next step of the assignment.

Ask which distinction best explains why some killings feel harder to justify:
1. intended harm vs foreseen harm
2. using a person as a means vs harming as a side effect
3. violating a right/constraint even for good results

Which of those seems to fit the cases best?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now at the key transition from exposition to critique. I should help them generate candidate non-consequentialist principles without naming a finished theory or composing their argument. The best move is to present a short menu of distinctions already implicit in trolley discussions and ask which one explains their intuitions best. This keeps it within the assignment and moves toward the 'right general account' question.

exchanges.student: ughh idk, maybe using-a-person-as-a-means? but then i’m not confident why that’s better than intended-vs-foreseen, they blur together for me fr.
exchanges.tutor:
They do overlap, but they’re not identical. Test them on a concrete pair instead of in the abstract.

In a footbridge-style case, is the person’s death part of your plan for stopping the trolley, or just a side effect you foresee? In a switch case, how is that different?

Answer those two questions in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is circling around two related distinctions. To prevent redundancy and move them forward, I should ask for a concrete comparison using one trolley-style contrast, because applying the concepts to a case will help separate them. I should also explicitly note the overlap without resolving it for them. Keep it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: i think in footbridge the death is part of the plan bc the body stops the trolley, and in switch it’s more like u redirect it and just know someone else will die. but idk how to turn that into the “right general account” part.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s the core insight. Now stop circling and turn it into a principle.

Try this structure in your own words:
- It is sometimes okay to kill one to save five when...
- It is not okay when the one person is...
- That is why act consequentialism gets ___ right/wrong, because it ignores...

Fill those three blanks without worrying about polished wording.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated a key distinction. Now I should help them convert that insight into a candidate general principle, but without wording it for them. Since we've spent several turns on the same idea, I should prompt them to integrate it into a rough thesis structure. I will ask for a fill-in-the-blank style conceptual outline rather than provide prose.

exchanges.student: maybe: it’s okay when the death is a side effect of saving the five, not okay when the person is used as the means, and act consequentialism gets some trolley cases wrong bc it ignores that distinction? i’m still not sure if that’s too simplistic tho.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a strong candidate, not too simplistic yet. Now do the real philosophical work: stress-test it.

Take Fuel and Blood one at a time and ask:
1. Is the person’s death a means or a side effect?
2. Does your principle give the verdict you want?

If one case comes out wrong, then your account needs revising. Which case worries you most?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a promising draft principle. I should validate its potential while pushing them to test it against the assignment's cases, since a good general account must handle Fuel, Blood, and trolley variants. I must not refine their wording for them or give verdicts. The next Socratic move is stress-testing with counterexamples.

exchanges.student: Blood worries me most tbh, bc i can’t even tell if the person is being used as a means there or if it’s just redescribing the same outcome.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—focus there. Ask one precise question: in Blood, does saving the five work because that person is killed, or would the five be saved even if that person somehow didn’t die?

If the death is doing essential causal work, that points one way; if not, it points the other. Which seems true in Blood, based on the lecture’s setup?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stuck on one case and needs a sharper diagnostic question, not the answer. I should help them distinguish 'means' from 'side effect' by asking whether the victim’s death plays a necessary causal role in saving the five. That moves directly toward evaluating Blood and keeps the response brief and Socratic.

transcripts.context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
transcripts.exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i'm stuck on the philosophy thing about killing 1 to save 5, and idk what act consequentialism is saying exactly. can u just explain it kinda broadly first?
exchanges.tutor:
Broadly: act consequentialism says the moral status of an action depends only on that act’s consequences. So in a case like “kill 1 to save 5,” it would ask: which available action leads to the best overall outcome?

A useful next step: in your own words, what do you think counts as the “best outcome” here—just the number of lives saved, or something more?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should give a brief, high-level orientation without supplying a full solution, since the student asked for a broad explanation. To follow the Socratic method and guided discovery, I will define the core idea of act consequentialism in minimal terms, connect it to the assignment, and then ask a focused question that moves toward applying it to the killing-one-to-save-five cases. I will keep it bite-sized and avoid writing any part of the student's answer for them.

exchanges.student: tbh idk, maybe just more lives saved? but then the trolley stuff starts feeling weird fast.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—that “weird fast” reaction is exactly the issue the assignment wants you to analyze.

If act consequentialism focuses on outcomes, what would it seem to say about two cases that both end with 1 dead and 5 saved? Should it treat them the same, or is there some difference you think matters?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified the basic act consequentialist intuition but is noticing tension with trolley cases. I should validate that this is the core philosophical pressure point, then guide them to distinguish cases by asking a comparison question that directs them toward applying the theory rather than giving conclusions. I will stay concise and avoid supplying the answer.

exchanges.student: yeah maybe it treats them the same, which is kinda why i'm confused lol. can u just go thru Fuel vs Blood super simply?
exchanges.tutor:
Super simply: compare them with 3 questions.
1. What action is available?
2. What happens if you do it vs don’t do it?
3. Is the one person harmed as a means, or just foreseen?

Now try this: what is the key factual difference between Fuel and Blood, in one sentence each?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should not provide a direct walkthrough with conclusions, because that would risk supplying the analysis. But the student is stuck, so I can simplify the comparison structure and prompt them to fill it in. This uses guided discovery, keeps the focus on the assignment, and moves toward evaluating whether act consequentialism gets the cases right.

exchanges.student: i'm honestly mixing them up rn, like i remember names but not the setup. that angle didn't help much; can u just remind me what happens in each?
exchanges.tutor:
Brief reminder only:

Fuel: you can redirect a threat/resource so that 5 are saved, but 1 person will die as a side effect.
Blood: you can take something from 1 unwilling person to save 5, and the 1 is more directly used for the rescue.

Don’t get hung up on the names. Bigger picture: which of those feels more like “using” the person, and why might that matter?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for case setups, not for the moral conclusions. Giving the bare factual scenarios is acceptable scaffolding because it helps memory without solving the assignment. I should keep it brief, avoid interpretive conclusions, and then immediately redirect with a question that advances analysis using the assignment’s central distinction.

exchanges.student: Blood feels more like using them, i guess. but fr i still want the bigger picture answer, like when is it actually okay overall?
exchanges.tutor:
That’s the big question of the assignment: is saving more lives enough, or are there limits on using a person as a means?

So test these two ideas:
1. “It’s okay whenever it produces the best outcome.”
2. “It’s not okay when the 1 is intentionally used as a tool.”

Which seems closer to your view so far, and can you think of a trolley case that supports it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now asking for the overarching view. I must not provide the final answer, but I can help them frame the competing principles and move them toward constructing their own general account. I should be explicit that this assignment asks them to assess act consequentialism and then propose a better general rule if needed. A focused either/or question will advance the solution without giving it away.

exchanges.student: probably 2, but i'm not super sure bc trolley switching still seems okay to me and pushing doesn't. ngl can u just give me a clean overall explanation of how to answer this prompt?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—use this structure, not a finished answer:

1. State what act consequentialism says: judge each act by its consequences.
2. Apply it: ask what it would say in cases where 1 dies and 5 live.
3. Test it on Fuel, Blood, switching, pushing: would it treat some of these alike?
4. Then give your criticism: does that match your intuitions?
5. Finally, propose your rule: what difference makes killing sometimes okay and sometimes not?

Your own view is already emerging: switching may be okay, pushing not. What principle explains that difference in one rough sentence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants a full roadmap, but I must not provide a complete solution or write the answer for them. I can give a concise structure for approaching the prompt, framed as steps and questions, which supports guided discovery and keeps them on-task. I will use their own intuition about switching vs pushing to organize the response, and end by asking them to produce a brief thesis attempt.

exchanges.student: maybe like, it's only okay if the person's death isn't the thing i'm using to save the 5? i'm not sure if that's the right wording tho.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a strong direction. Don’t worry about perfect wording yet—worry about whether the idea survives the cases.

Test your idea on two contrasts:
- switching vs pushing
- Fuel vs Blood

In which case is the person’s death part of the plan, and in which is it more like a side effect?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is forming the key principle. I should affirm the conceptual progress without polishing their wording, since I must not write for them. To keep the Socratic method, I will ask them to test their own draft principle against the assignment cases, which moves directly toward a defensible final position. I will keep it brief.

exchanges.student: i think pushing and Blood are part of the plan, and switching/Fuel are more side effect. but idk if that fully answers the “right general account” part.
exchanges.tutor:
You’re very close. The “general account” is just your rule stated at a higher level.

Try this move: instead of listing cases, say what feature makes an act not okay. What is the forbidden thing—killing as a means? intentionally using a person? making their death part of the rescue plan?

Pick one as your core idea and say it in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has reached the core distinction and is asking how to make it into a general account. I should push them to generalize from the cases into a rule, without drafting the wording for them. This maintains guided discovery and keeps the answer bite-sized. A targeted question about what makes an act impermissible will help them formulate the account themselves.

exchanges.student: maybe: it's not morally okay to kill 1 to save 5 when the 1 is being intentionally used as the means of saving the 5, but it can be okay when the death is a foreseen side effect.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a strong core view. Now make sure you do two things with it:

1. Contrast it with act consequentialism: why would act consequentialism resist that distinction?
2. Apply your principle to the lecture cases: which ones come out okay, which not?

Write a 3-part outline in your own words: act consequentialism, why it seems wrong, your general account.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a promising thesis-like general account. I should affirm its philosophical strength, but I must not polish, revise, or provide a final written answer. To keep the Socratic method, I should now direct them to integrate what we discussed into their own response structure, since the conversation has focused on the same idea for several turns. I will ask them to test the principle against act consequentialism and the lecture cases, which advances completion of the assignment without supplying prose.

exchanges.student: ugh i'm still fuzzy tbh—can u just summarize act consequentialism vs my view in like 2 super plain sentences?
exchanges.tutor: Act consequentialism says the right act is whichever produces the best overall results, so saving five at the cost of one will often look justified. Your view says outcomes are not the only thing that matter, because it can be wrong to intentionally use one person as the means of saving the five, even if more lives are saved.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants a very brief contrast, which I can provide at a high level without turning it into a ready-made submission. I will keep it to two plain sentences, avoid polished essay language, and directly support the assignment by highlighting the key disagreement: outcomes alone versus the means/side-effect distinction.
